From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Indian sports award

| Arjuna Award | |
| --- | --- |
| Civilian award for outstanding contributions to Sports (Individual/Team) | |
| [Mohammed Shami](/wiki/Mohammed_Shami "Mohammed Shami") receiving the Arjuna Award in 2024 | |
| Awarded for | Sports honour in [India](/wiki/India "India") |
| Sponsored by | [Government of India](/wiki/Government_of_India "Government of India") |
| Reward | [₹](/wiki/Indian_rupee "Indian rupee")15,00,000[[1]](#cite_note-1) |
| First award | 1961 |
| Final award | 2024 |
| Highlights | |
| Total awarded | 967 individuals + 1 team award |
| Precedence | |
| Next (higher) | Major Dhyan Chand Khel Ratna |
| [Major Dhyan Chand Khel Ratna](/wiki/Major_Dhyan_Chand_Khel_Ratna "Major Dhyan Chand Khel Ratna") → | |

Arjuna award recipients[[2]](#cite_note-ArjunaAward-2)

| Year | Number of recipients |
| --- | --- |
| 1961–1969 | 95(+ 1 team award) |
| 1970–1979 | 136 |
| 1980–1989 | 147 |
| 1990–1999 | 147 |
| 2000–2009 | 151 |
| 2010–2019 | 178 |
| 2020–2029 | 119 |

The **Arjuna Award**, officially known as **Arjuna Awards given for Outstanding Performance in Sports and Games**,[[3]](#cite_note-Scheme-3) is the second-highest sporting honour of [India](/wiki/India "India"), the highest being the Major Dhyan Chand [Khel Ratna Award](/wiki/Khel_Ratna_Award "Khel Ratna Award"). The award is named after [Arjuna](/wiki/Arjuna "Arjuna"), one of the characters of the [Sanskrit](/wiki/Sanskrit "Sanskrit") [epic](/wiki/Indian_epic_poetry "Indian epic poetry") *[Mahabharata](/wiki/Mahabharata "Mahabharata")* of [ancient India](/wiki/History_of_India "History of India").[[4]](#cite_note-4) In [Hinduism](/wiki/Hinduism "Hinduism"), he has been seen as a symbol of hard work, dedication and concentration.[[5]](#cite_note-5) It is awarded annually by the [Ministry of Youth Affairs and Sports](/wiki/Ministry_of_Youth_Affairs_and_Sports "Ministry of Youth Affairs and Sports"). Before the introduction of the [Major Dhyan Chand Khel Ratna](/wiki/Major_Dhyan_Chand_Khel_Ratna "Major Dhyan Chand Khel Ratna") in 1991–1992, the Arjuna award was the highest sporting honour of India.[[6]](#cite_note-EverestNews-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7) The nominations for the award are received from all government-recognised national sports federations, the [Indian Olympic Association](/wiki/Indian_Olympic_Association "Indian Olympic Association"), the [Sports Authority of India](/wiki/Sports_Authority_of_India "Sports Authority of India") (SAI), the Sports Promotion and Control Boards, the state and the union territory governments and the Major Dhyan Chandra Khel Ratna, Arjuna, [Dhyan Chand](/wiki/Dhyan_Chand_Award "Dhyan Chand Award") and [Dronacharya](/wiki/Dronacharya_Award "Dronacharya Award") awardees of the previous years. The recipients are selected by a committee constituted by the Ministry and are honoured for their "good performance in the field of sports over a period of four years" at the international level and for having shown "qualities of leadership, sportsmanship and a sense of discipline." As of 2020[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Arjuna_Award&action=edit), the award comprises "a [bronze](/wiki/Bronze "Bronze") [statuette](/wiki/Statuette "Statuette") of Arjuna, certificate, ceremonial dress, and a cash prize of ₹15 lakh (US$18,000)."[[a]](#cite_note-a-8)

Instituted in 1961 to honour the outstanding sportspersons of the country,[[8]](#cite_note-9) the award over the years has undergone a number of expansions, reviews and rationalizations. The award was expanded to include all the recognised sporting disciplines in 1977, introduced indigenous games and physically handicapped categories in 1995 and introduced a lifetime contribution category in 1995 leading to creation of a separate [Dhyan Chand Award](/wiki/Dhyan_Chand_Award "Dhyan Chand Award") in 2002.[[9]](#cite_note-10)[[10]](#cite_note-11) The latest revision in 2018 stipulates that the award is given only to the disciplines included in the events like [Olympic Games](/wiki/Olympic_Games "Olympic Games"), [Paralympic Games](/wiki/Paralympic_Games "Paralympic Games"), [Asian Games](/wiki/Asian_Games "Asian Games"), [Commonwealth Games](/wiki/Commonwealth_Games "Commonwealth Games"), [World Championship](/wiki/World_championship "World championship") and [World Cup](/wiki/World_cup_competition "World cup competition") along with [cricket](/wiki/Cricket "Cricket"), indigenous games, and [Parasports](/wiki/Disabled_sports "Disabled sports"). It also recommends giving only fifteen awards in a year, relaxing in case of excellent performance in major [multi-sport events](/wiki/Multi-sport_event "Multi-sport event"), team sports, across gender and giving away of at least one award in the physically challenged category.[[3]](#cite_note-Scheme-3)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Arjuna_Award&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Since the year 2001, the award is given only in disciplines falling under the following categories:

* Olympic Games / Asian Games / Commonwealth Games / World Cup / World Championship Disciplines and Cricket
* Indigenous Games
* Sports for the Physically Challenged

## Explanatory notes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Arjuna_Award&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Explanatory notes")]

1. **[^](#cite_ref-a_8-0)** The cash prize was introduced in the year 1977–1978 as a scholarship of ₹200 (US$2.40) a month for 2 years.[[11]](#cite_note-12) It was revised to one time cash prize of ₹5,000 (US$59) in 1986,[[12]](#cite_note-13) to ₹20,000 (US$240) in 1987,[[13]](#cite_note-14) to ₹50,000 (US$590) in 1993,[[14]](#cite_note-15) to ₹1.5 lakh (US$1,800) in 1998,[[15]](#cite_note-16) to ₹3 lakh (US$3,500) in 2001,[[16]](#cite_note-17) to ₹5 lakh (US$5,900) in 2009,[[17]](#cite_note-18) and to ₹15 lakh (US$18,000) in 2020.[[18]](#cite_note-19)
